risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following the initial assessment, each child was randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions." Comment: Although the article does not spell out the exact randomization mechanism, it explicitly states that participants were randomly assigned to treatment groups, and the report provides additional context consistent with a genuine random process (four treatment arms, siblings assigned to the same condition). Under the permissive rule for this domain, this is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: Following the initial assessment, each child was randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions.
_evidence.method_summary: Children were randomly assigned after the initial assessment to one of four treatment conditions.
context_clues: randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions
randomly assigned to the four groups
randomly assigned to receive 8 or 16 treatment sessions

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following the initial assessment, each child was randomly assigned to one of the four treatment conditions... Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session." Comment: The article states that assignment was random and that the project coordinator was blind after assignment, but it does not describe any mechanism used to conceal the upcoming allocation from the person enrolling participants before enrollment. There is no explicit central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers, or other concealment procedure reported, so allocation concealment cannot be confirmed and remains unclear.

_evidence.note: The article describes random assignment and states that the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, but it does not describe how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the recruiter.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session." Comment: Participants were explicitly informed of their allocation by the therapist, so participants were not blinded; personnel delivering treatment (therapists) necessarily knew the assigned condition because they had to provide different components (e.g., trauma narrative vs no trauma narrative). The study also reports many subjective outcomes (e.g., parent- and child-report symptom scales), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session. Randomly selected tapes from different stages of treatment were also reviewed by independent raters who were blind to the assigned treatment type further confirming that narratives were not written in the no narrative conditions.
_evidence.blinding_method: No participant/personnel blinding described; project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment; independent raters were blind to assigned treatment type; participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The project coordinator at each site provided a detailed explanation of the study ... Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session." Comment: The coordinator was blinded, but participants were explicitly unblinded to allocation and the study included multiple patient- and parent-reported outcomes (e.g., BDI-II, CBCL, CSBI, PERQ, PPQ, CDI, Fear, MASC, Shame), which are susceptible to detection bias because the patient/parent is the assessor. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of these unblinded subjective/self-report outcomes makes the overall domain high risk even though some K-SADS outcomes were assessed by blinded independent raters.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Because the project coordinator was blind to treatment assignment, participants were told of the treatment assignment by the therapist during the first treatment session. Randomly selected tapes from different stages of treatment were also reviewed by independent raters who were blind to the assigned treatment type further confirming that narratives were not written in the no narrative conditions.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "16 (8%) children and their parents never returned after being assigned to treatment, and 15 (7%) children and their parents left after attending only one or two sessions. These 31 (15%) children and their parents were defined as dropouts. Therefore, 179 (85%) children attended at least three TF-CBT sessions. This sample of completers was used for the subsequent analyses." Comment: The paper reports observed post-randomization dropout and shows it was 15% overall, with no meaningful arm imbalance in the extracted per-arm flow (40/40, 39/39, 35/35, 44/44 analysed; lost 0 in each arm at the analysed time point). The authors also state that ITT analyses were run and gave no statistical differences versus the completer-based analyses, and they used mixed models/multiple imputation, which supports low risk for attrition bias on the outcomes of interest.

_evidence.missing_data_method: modified intent-to-treat; MM-ANCOVA; multiple imputation; complete-case
_evidence.notes: Primary analyses used only participants with 3 or more sessions: 'This sample of completers was used for the subsequent analyses.' Total loss 15% refers to dropout before 3 sessions ('16 (8%) ... never returned' and '15 (7%) ... left after attending only one or two sessions'). Table 1/2 indicate analysed Ns by arm for the post-treatment outcomes.
